coredata.dc:description: A composite based on Ag and carbon quantum dot (CQDs) doped bismuth metal organic framework (CAU-17) was synthesized by a one-step thermal solvent in situ growth. The microstructure, chemical composition, morphology, photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and photocatalytic activity of the composite were characterized. The produced composite with its unique energy band structure, enhances the visible light absorption and effectively delays the recombination of the photogenerated carriers. On the other hand, the modification with CQDs increases the concentration and transport rate of photogenerated carriers mainly attributed to their superior electron transport capacity and light trapping ability. The photocatalytic antibacterial effect of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs against common Gram-positive, Gram-negative bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli) and drug-resistant bacteria (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus), as well as its inhibition against HepG2 tumor cell were investigated. The results showed that CAU-17/Ag/CQDs exhibited a photocatalytic antibacterial effect with an inactivation rate as high as 99.9 %. At the low dose (0.2 mg/mL), CAU-17/Ag/CQDs indicated a significant inhibition against bacterial growth 20 min after visible light exposure, whereas at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, CAU-17/Ag/CQDs completely killed all the tested bacteria. At the concentration of 0.8 mg/mL, the inhibition rate against HepG2 tumor cells reached 75 %. The excellent photocatalytic property of the as prepared composite contributed to the doping of Ag and CQDs, which fundamentally altered the morphology and energy band distribution. Such a composite can be developed into an effective photocatalytic disinfection system and applied to water purification systems, biofilm rejection, combating different antibiotic resistances, and tumor therapy.

head.ce:title: Study of bismuth metal organic skeleton composites with photocatalytic antibacterial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the case of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, upon irradiation of the visible light, electrons in CAU-17/Ag can be excited from VB to CB. Owing to the high specific surface area and rich pore structure of the composite, the photogenerated electrons on the surface of CAU-17/Ag can be easily transferred to CQDs and be stabilized, consequently the chance for the recombination of electron-hole pair is greatly reduced. Instead, photogenerated electrons and holes may react with oxygen and water in the air to produce large amounts of ROS, •OH, and •O2 –. These active substances induced by CAU-17/Ag/CQDs can directly attack bacterial cell membrane, cell envelope or intrinsiccell structures to impose rather a non-specific oxidative stress that potentially leads to the cell death.

A composite based on Ag and carbon quantum dot (CQDs) doped bismuth metal organic framework (CAU-17) was synthesized by a one-step thermal solvent in situ growth. The microstructure, chemical composition, morphology, photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and photocatalytic activity of the composite were characterized. The produced composite with its unique energy band structure, enhances the visible light absorption and effectively delays the recombination of the photogenerated carriers. On the other hand, the modification with CQDs increases the concentration and transport rate of photogenerated carriers mainly attributed to their superior electron transport capacity and light trapping ability. The photocatalytic antibacterial effect of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs against common Gram-positive, Gram-negative bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli) and drug-resistant bacteria (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus), as well as its inhibition against HepG2 tumor cell were investigated. The results showed that CAU-17/Ag/CQDs exhibited a photocatalytic antibacterial effect with an inactivation rate as high as 99.9 %. At the low dose (0.2 mg/mL), CAU-17/Ag/CQDs indicated a significant inhibition against bacterial growth 20 min after visible light exposure, whereas at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, CAU-17/Ag/CQDs completely killed all the tested bacteria. At the concentration of 0.8 mg/mL, the inhibition rate against HepG2 tumor cells reached 75 %. The excellent photocatalytic property of the as prepared composite contributed to the doping of Ag and CQDs, which fundamentally altered the morphology and energy band distribution. Such a composite can be developed into an effective photocatalytic disinfection system and applied to water purification systems, biofilm rejection, combating different antibiotic resistances, and tumor therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute and chronic wounds caused by various injuries, including penetrating, impact, burns, and blunt injuries, cause long-term pain and related physical damages. Bacterial infection is known to cause serious complications and nerve and blood vessel injuries leading to chronic disease, and even death. The struggle against bacterial infections has been ongoing throughout human history. Bacterial infections are not only responsible for a large number of deaths but also cause serious social problems and economic losses. On the other hand, due to the abuse of antibiotics, many bacterial strands have developed drug resistance.[1–3]. Such a challenge demands the discovery and practice of new antibacterial agents with low or no drug resistance.

Photocatalysis has been most often applied to solve the energy crisis such as in photocatalytic hydrogen and oxygen production, CO2 reduction, and remedy of environmental pollution. In recent years, with its advanced oxidation nature, photocatalysis has also been used in water disinfection due to its ability to kill bacteria [4–6]. As an emerging antimicrobial technology, photocatalysis indicates its advantages, as the efficacy of bacterial inactivation as well as the cost can be controlled through an intelligent design of the photocatalysts [7–9].

Metal-organic framework materials have become the center of attention in the designing of catalysts owing to their high specific surface area, high porosity, good structural stability, and diverse structures [10]. Recently, nodules containing pathogenic metal ions (such as Ag+, Co2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+) have been introduced to the antimicrobial materials considering their relatively lower toxicity than that of the free metal ions in solution [11–13]. Bismuth compounds, bismuth salicylate, and colloidal bismuth citrate for instance, are the key components in the medicine to treat stomach diseases. Bismuth compounds have been used in pharmaceutical preparations for hundreds of years (since the 18th century) [14]. Taking advantage of bismuth such as high abundance, low relative toxicity compared to other heavy elements, unique electron pair effects, flexible coordination geometry, and potential for modulation of the structure of photophysical properties [15], various bismuth-based nanostructures have been developed from BiMOF (CAU-17) through hydrothermal and in situ electrochemical transformations and used as electrocatalysts [16], photocatalysts [17] and electrode materials [18]. However, CAU-17 based photocatalysts suffer from the drawback of short lived photogenerated electron-hole pairs leading to low photocatalytic efficiency [19]. Therefore, various strategies have been proposed to enhance the efficiencies of CAU-17, among which are microstructural modulation [20–22], hybridization with other materials [23–25], and element doping [26,27]. Meanwhile, precious metal-based nanocomposites usually prolong photo-induced carrier separation through surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [28] and larger light absorption efficiency. For example, palladium, gold, and silver nanoparticles can produce free radicals and reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon direct contact with bacteria [29–31], which leads to bacterial cell death either through oxidative stress or by destroying the osmotic balance [32]. Herein we doped Ag to enhance the photocatalytic properties of CAU-17, as Ag has shown multifaceted potential in biosensors, drug retardants, antibacterial, photoacoustic, antitumor, and biomedical imaging [33].

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been emerging with excellent optical properties [34], good biocompatibility [35], high water solubility [36], photostability [37] and their tunable optical properties [38], light-mediated thermal [39], photoluminescence [40], upconversion properties [41] and ROS (•O2 −, H2O2, and •OH) generation capabilities, hence CQDs have been introduced to the materials for photocatalysis [42], cell imaging [43], drug delivery [44], photoelectric conversion [45], chemical/biosensing [46], supercapacitors [47] and cancer therapy [48]. In particular, Ren et al [49] synthesized CQDs-modified Bi2WO6 by a simple hydrothermal method, and the obtained CQDs/Bi2WO6 complexes exhibited photocatalytic activities 4.01 and 64.71 times higher than those of pure Bi2WO6 and CQDs. The heterojunction constructed by coupling CQDS with BiOCl by Xu et al [50] forms abundant oxygen vacancies and greatly improves the photogenerated charge separation rate. In addition to this, the combination of Bi doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with liposomes can also be used for anti-tumor visualization therapy [51], so we chose CQDs to enhance the photo catalytically active antimicrobial capacity of CAU-17, and at the same time to explore the potential of the composites for anti-tumor applications.

In the photocatalytic pathway, active species such as holes (h+), superoxide (•O2 −), and hydroxyl radical (•OH) play a decisive role in the inhibition of bacteria. While the antimicrobial mechanism led by photocatalysis remains unknown, it is generally believed due to the generated ROS under the irradiation of light that imposes oxidative stress targeting multiple locations including cell membrane, cell envelop, cell wall, and intrinsic cell structures, and eventually leads to bacterial cell death [52]. There has been no report on materials that can simultaneously be antibacterial and tumor suppressors. In this study, we prepared a new ternary heterojunction material (CAU-17/Ag/CQDs) with excellent photocatalytic properties by modifying CAU-17 with the noble metals Ag and CQDs, which were grown in situ on the interlayer surface of CAU-17 by a solvothermal method. The introduction of CQDs can stabilize the photogenerated carriers and improve the transfer efficiency of photogenerated carriers. The doping of silver and CQDs led to the increase of CAU-17 layer spacing, which broadened the area of CAU-17 light absorption, and also solved the problem of uneven distribution of nanoparticles, which effectively enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the composites making CAU-17/Ag/CQDs show better antimicrobial and anticancer activities under light. This work provides a new idea to improve the photocatalytic performance of metal MOF photocatalysts. Finally, the photocatalytic performance was evaluated using UV–Vis, EIS, PC, ICP-MS, and EPR.

Sodium chloride（NaCl, 99.5 %）、bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O, 99 %)、absolute ethyl alcohol（AE, 99.7 %）、silver nitrate（AgNO3, 99.8 %）(Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd), tannin（TA, 95 %）、polyvinylpyrrolidone K27-K30 (PVP, Mw 55000) (Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Company), agar powder (Beijing Aoboxing Biotechnology Co., Ltd), beef extract (biochemical reagent) (Beijing Shuangxuan Microbial Culture Medium Products Factory), peptone (biochemical reagent) (Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), trimesic acid (H3BTC, 98 %), polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw:1800) (Shanghai McLean Industrial Company) and organic solvents methanol (MeOH,99.5 %) N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF,99.5 %)(Anlong Bohua (Tianjin) Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., Ltd.) are purchased and used without purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Bacterial strains were purchased from Lanzhou Maternal and Child Health Hospital. All strains (S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA) were streaked on LB plates, and incubated at 37 ℃ for 24 h. One colony was taken from each culture plate, inoculated in fresh sterilized LB solution (5 mL), followed by incubation at 37℃ overnight. The broth after overnight incubation was further diluted to about 105 colony forming units (CFU.mL −1). The concentration of bacteria used for antibacterial detection is approximately 105 CFU.mL −1. Three groups of parallel experiments were carried out for each bacteriostatic process.

The results were analyzed using) scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), transient photocurrent test (PC), Mott-Schottky curve, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), nitrogen adsorption–desorption (BET), UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) were used to characterize the materials. Experimental details of the characterization techniques used in this study are described in the Supporting Information document.

The carbon dots were prepared using a method slightly modified from that described in the literature [53]. Dissolved 1.5 g of TA and 1.5 g of PEI (1.5 g) in 110 mL of distilled water and stirred for 30 min. The prepared solution was transferred to a 150 mL autoclave for hydrothermal reaction at 150 °C for 300 min. The product of the hydrothermal reaction was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant containing N-CDS was collected. The reddish brown solution was dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (cut-off molecular weight 1000 Da) for 2 days to remove the low molecular weight compounds. The N-CD-containing solution was filtered through a membrane with a pore size of 0.45 µm and the final product of CQDs was obtained through freeze-drying.

Accordion-shaped bismuth MOF (CAU-17) was synthesized using a method slightly modified from that in the literature [54]. Dissolve 0.84056 g H3BTC and 0.2425 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O in a mixture of 40 mL methanol and 20 mL DMF with low power sonication until the solution became clear. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL PTFE reactor and stayed at 120℃for 24 h. The obtained solid phase, CAU-17 was then washed with DMF and methanol several times along with a centrifuge, and the product of CAU-17 was obtained by drying at 70 ℃ for 12 h. The CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites were prepared through an in situ growth method using PVP as the dispersant, and the same synthesis scheme is described in scheme 1 . The detailed preparation method is in the supporting information.

HepG2 cells in good growth condition and logarithmic phase were tested for cell survival and growth using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The concentration of cell suspension was adjusted and inoculated into 96-well plates with 100 μL of cell suspension per well. The plates were transferred to a 5 % incubator and the cells were incubated at 37℃ for 24 h for apposition growth. 100 μL of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposites at concentrations of 0.025 mg/mL, 0.05 mg/mL, 0.1 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.4 mg/mL and 0.8 mg/mL were added respectively and incubation was continued for 48 h. Finally, 100 μL of MTT solution was added to each well and the incubation was continued for 4 h. The culture solution was removed 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well and the absorbance was measured by ELISA after 10 min of shaking. The inhibition rate of cells by CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposites was calculated as:

The crystallinity of the material was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, whereas the surface functional group and bonding information were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. XRD pattern (Fig. 1 a) for the prepared CAU-17 indicates some strong diffraction peaks at 8.1°, 9.8°, 10.3° and 12.5° corresponding to (030), (240) and (111) faces of CAU-17 [55], indicating the successful synthesis of CAU-17. These characteristic diffraction peaks for CAU-17/CQDs shift to a large angular 2 θ , implying that CQDs are successfully doped on CAU-17. Compared to CAU-17, three diffraction peaks experience a slight shift to larger 2 θ for CAU-17/Ag, an indication of the interaction between Ag ions and the basis of the Bi-MOF leading to lattice distortion [56]. The difference in peak intensities is due to disordered pore-clogging intermediates/precursors (Figure S2） [57,58]. Overall, the characteristic diffraction peak intensities for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are lower than those for CAU-17 due to the reduced crystallinity of Bi-MOF by the doping of silver ions and CQDs [59]. Through Bragg equation: 2dsinθ = nλ(d: crystal plane spacing (interlayer spacing); λ: X-ray wavelength (first order diffraction); Cu target Ka ray (λ = 0.15406 nm) the interlayer spacing was calculated for each material (Figure S1) [60], which are in an order of CAU-17 < CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, Such a interlayer spacing order indicates that the doping of silver ions and CQDs renders enlarging of interlayer spacing, which can also be observed in the SEM images in Fig. 2 .

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b) of all materials indicate a broad band in the wavenumber range from 3400 cm−1 to 2600 cm−1 representing OH stretching vibration on a carboxylic group of CAU-17. Two peaks observed in 1730–1680 cm−1 are assigned as CO stretching vibration of two kinds of CO groups, one at a relatively higher frequency is identified as free CO in H3BTC and the one at a relatively lower frequency is recognized as CO engaged in the interaction with Bi3+ in CAU-17. The relative intensity of these two CO stretching absorption peaks depends on different species, For the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite, the peak at a relatively lower frequency has a much larger intensity than the peak at a relatively higher frequency indicating that CO functional group is in a more complicated interactions involving Ag+ and CQDs in the composite. For all the species, absorption bands are observed at 1349 cm−1, and 736 cm−1 representing CO stretching and OH bending vibrations. Moreover, the absorption band at 537 cm−1 for all four species is identified as Bi-O stretching vibration [61]. The splitting of the CO absorption band for all the species containing CAU-17 implies that the COOH functional group on H3BTC is electrostatically coordinated to the bismuth, and the dependence of the relative intensity of two split bands indicates that Ag+ and CQDs are successfully doped on CAU-17.

The morphology of the CAU-17 and its evolution upon doping with Ag+ and CQDs are depicted in Fig. 2. An accordion-like morphology is observed for CAU-17. With the addition of CQDs and Ag ions, the interval between accordion layers generally increases. The magnified images for the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites show that the morphology of CAU-17 doped with CQDs and Ag+ transforms into multiple thin sheets with the thickness in nanometers, on which the particles of CQDs and Ag are deposited. Unlike the accordion morphology of CAU-17 wherein the nanosheets are closely piled on one another, the nanosheets develop a larger gap for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites. Such a lamellar structure helps to form a large contact area among CAU-17, Ag, and CQDs, which effectively stabilizes the photoinduced carriers and delays their recombination [62–64].

TEM images of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (Fig. 3 a) show that the sheet is layered on CAU-17, and large and small ag particles are uniformly dispersed on the sheet. Fig. 3b shows that there are two different sizes of particles, the larger particles have a diameter of about 20 nm the smaller ones have a diameter below 5 nm, and the small particles are coated with larger particles. High resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) analysis was performed to distinguish between these two types of particles, and Fig. 3c reveals two well-defined lattice spacings of 0.23 and 0.34 nm, which can coincide with the (111) face of Ag and (Fig. 3d) CQDs respectively (002) faces, indicating that heterojunctions have indeed formed between them, confirming the successful growth of Ag and CQDs on the CAU-17 surface [65]. The absence of Ag-related diffraction peaks in the XRD patterns of CAU-17/Ag samples and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs is attributed to the overlapping peaks of CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs at about 24 to 46 degrees and the relatively low content of Ag diffraction peaks that are too low in intensity [66]. The above characterization confirms that we have successfully synthesized CAU-17/Ag/CQDs ternary hybrid materials. It is expected that the nanocomposite CAU-17/Ag/CQDs with the presence of small and dispersed CQDs and Ag nanoparticles in the CAU-17 layer can exhibit effective antimicrobial activity.

XPS was performed to further identify the bonding status of surface elements of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs. The survey spectra (Fig. 4 a) confirm the presence of C, O, and Bi in CAU-17 and C, N, O, Bi, and Ag in CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, and the absence of peaks of other elements in the spectra indicates the high purity of the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites. High resolution of Bi 4f spectra (Fig. 4b) indicate XPS peaks for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (Bi 4f5/2:165.08 eV and Bi 4f7/2:159.74 eV) move to lower binding energies relative to those for CAU-17 (Bi 4f5/2: 165.29 eV and Bi 4f7/2:159.89 eV) of CAU-17 respectively, which means the doping of Ag ions and CQDs affects the electronic structure of bismuth, and the electron density in the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites increases upon doping [67]. The binding energy difference of 5.4 eV between Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2 indicates that the valence state of Bi is + 3 in the CAU-17/Ag /CQDs composites [68]. The overall percentage of Bi exhibits some change from CAU-17 (3.56 %) to CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (2.00 %), which confirms the doping of Ag and CQDs.

Three peaks at 284.5 eV, 287.2 eV, and 289.0 eV in the high resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 4c) for CAU-17 are assigned as the C in the form of CC/CC, CO, and CO respectively, whereas corresponding peaks show up at 284.80 eV, 286.39 eV, and 288.61 eV respectively for CAU-17/Ag /CQDs representing the C in the form of CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO respectively [69] The binding energies for all forms of C1s shift upon doping implying the boing status are slightly changed due to the interactions introduced by doping [87].

The high resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 4d) can be de-convoluted into two components at 599.66 eV and 531.69 for CAU-17 representing the O in the form of CO and Bi-O respectively. However, upon doping the peak representing the O in the form of CO shifts to 533.38 eV, which is 0.28 eV lower than that for CAU-17, implying that the CO group plays a key role in the connection with the doped components [70]. Two distinct peaks at 374.39 eV and 368.31 eV are identified for Ag 3d in CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (Fig. 4e) representing Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 respectively. The difference of 6.0 eV between these two peaks is evidence of the presence of metallic Ag ions [71].

The high-resolution map of N1s for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (Fig. 4f) indicates four peaks at 403.00 eV, 401.69 eV, 399.80 eV, and 397.45 eV, corresponding to graphitic nitrogen, pyrrole nitrogen, amino nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen of CQDs, respectively [72]. In the FT-IR spectrum of the composite, no obvious absorption band was identified for N-containing functional groups due to the low containing of the CQDs and rather high intensities of the bands representing the functional groups on CAU-17.

The camera images in Fig. 5 (a-c) and the bacterial survival histograms in Fig. 5(d-f) represent the bactericidal effect of saline, CQDs, CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, CAU-17/Ag/CQDs treatment of Staphylococcus aureus (S.aures), Escherichia coli (E.coli) and methicillin-resistant Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), respectively under dark conditions and light conditions. For each strand of bacteria, the antibacterial effect is much less obvious in dark conditions than in light. The light sensitivity of the antimicrobial effect of four species shows an order of CQDs < CAU-17 < CAU-17/CQDs ≈ CAU-17/Ag ≈ CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, wherein, the antimicrobial activity of the ternary material CAU-17/Ag/CQDs under light or in dark is the most significant, with almost 100 % bactericidal efficiency against S. aureus, E. coli and MRSA in light, which concludes that CAU-17/Ag/CQDs had significant photocatalytic bactericidal activity.

The concentration dependence of the antibacterial effect of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs (Fig. 6a) indicates that at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, the growth of all three strands of bacteria is completely inhibited. The SEM images (Fig. 6 b) of S. aureus, E.coli and MRSA control cells and those after treatment of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs in dark for 24 h and in light for 20 mins indicate no obvious morphology change of the cells treated in dark, however, obvious sunken, atrophied, fragmentation of the cell wall and destruction of cell integrity are observed for the cells of both bacterial strands after the treatment of composite in light for 20 mins. Such an observation further confirms the photocatalytic antibacterial activity of the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite.

Table S1 compares the results we obtained with the previously reported inhibition results of bacteriostatic MOFs. The ternary heterojunction material CAU-17/Ag/CQDs prepared in this study can kill not only common Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria but also drug-resistant bacteria under light, and it also has a synergistic effect of tumor inhibition. The CAU-17/Ag/CQDs ternary heterojunction composites have higher photocatalytic performance than the simple MOF materials, and the slow release of Ag+, and Bi3+ make the catalysts have long-term bacterial inhibition and synergistic bacterial inhibition effect, which can kill bacteria better. Meanwhile, the antimicrobial mechanism of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs makes it possible for bacteria not to develop resistance, making it a promising bactericide with potential for photodynamic therapy.

The transfer separation behavior and optical absorption properties of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite carriers were investigated using a combination of electrochemical experiments and optical analysis. CAU-17/Ag/CQDs shows a much higher transient photocurrent response of the photocatalysts (Fig. 7 a) than CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/CQDs, whereas the transient photocurrent response is the lowest for CAU-17 indicating doping, particularly doping with Ag and CQDs simultaneously greatly elongates the lifetime of photogenerated electrons and holes and accelerates their transferring [73]. Based on the comparison of photocurrent between CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, it is realized introduction of CQDs greatly stabilizes the photogenerated carriers and enhances their transfer efficiency by altering the morphology of CAU-17/Ag. The electrochemical impedance plot (Fig. 8 b) shows the magnitude of carrier transfer resistance of CAU17/Ag/CQDs, and the specific charge transfer resistance (Rct) values of each material were calculated using an equivalent circuit (Figure S5a), and the fitted values of Rct for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs were significantly smaller than those of pure CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, and CAU-17/Ag (Figure S5b), thus demonstrating the excellent photocatalytic activity of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites [74]. It is likely the deposition of CQDs on the nanosheet of CAU-17/Ag greatly enlarges specific surface area, which means more abundant transport pathways for photogenerated carriers, a key factor in photocatalysis [75].

Compared to CAU-17, the absorptions (Fig. 5c) of all the doped species indicate a broad peak in the range from 350 nm to 650 nm, wherein the absorption intensity is in an order of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs > CAU-17/Ag > CAU-17/CQDs. This absorption band raised due to doping was identified as a spin forbidden band in the work of Miao et al [76]. The strong labeled absorption edges (Fig. 5d) of CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs were located at 335, 378, 406 and 532 nm, respectively, from which the corresponding band gap of the semiconductor can be calculated by the upcoming Tacu diagram equation αhν = A(hv-Eg) n
/2 (Α: absorption coefficient, h: Planck's constant, A: a constant dependent on the electron-hole mobility, n/2: sample energy band, hv: photon energy, Eg: optical band gap energy). The estimated Eg for CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are 3.71 eV, 2.95 eV, 2.77 eV, and 1.31 eV respectively. Doping enhances the absorption of visible light and reduces the band gap indicating that the light-trapping ability of the photocatalyst was enhanced. Theoretically, the forbidden bandwidth is an important parameter to measure the redox ability of a semiconductor. The forbidden bandwidth for CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, the smallest among the four species, is greater than 1.23 eV, the minimum value of the forbidden band width for water decomposition [77].

The Mott-Schottky curves of CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs have negative slopes, so they are all P-type semiconductors (Figure S5c). The flat band potentials (Vfb) of CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs for Ag/AgCl electrodes were 1.95 V, 1.18 V, 1.09 V, and 1.05 V, respectively (Relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (vs NHE): ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.1976 (25 °C), ENHE is equivalent to 2.15 V, 1.38 V, 1.29 V and 1.25 V.). For P-type semiconductors, the ECB is typically positive by 0.1–0.3 V compared to VfB, depending on the active mass of the electrons and the carrier concentration, and the difference in our study was set to 0.2 V [78,79]. Thus, the ECB of CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are about − 1.75 V, − 0.98 V, − 0.89 V, and − 0.68 V, respectively. In addition, the EVB of the complex can be calculated by the formula EVB = ECB + Eg, where EVB and ECB are the valence band edge potential and the conduction band edge potential [80]. Thus, the EVB values of CAU-17, CAU-17/CQDs, CAU-17/Ag, and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are 1.96 V, 1.97 V, 1.88 V, and 0.63 V respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of mechanism of photocatalytic bacteriostasis
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most common mechanisms for the bactericidal activity of silver-loaded nanomaterials is the release of Ag+ and the generation of ROS [81]. In order to determine the possible bactericidal mechanism of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, the main active species generated in the photocatalytic system of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs were investigated by dark light comparison experiments. From the ESR spectra (Fig. 8a and b), the •O2 − and •OH radicals could be captured by DMPO in methanol and aqueous dispersion, respectively. Both DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − signals were clearly observed under visible light irradiation. However, the characteristic signal peaks of •O2 − and •OH radicals were almost absent in the dark, indicating that•O2 − and •OH radicals were generated during the photocatalytic process.

The release of Ag+ and Bi3+ ions contributes to sterilization because they can denature proteins in cell membranes, penetrate the membranes, and cause bacterial cell damage, and silver exposure may have implications for human health. We analyzed the time-dependent changes of Ag+ and Bi3+ concentrations released from water under vigorous agitation for 1 h, 6 h, 24 h, and 36 h, as shown in Fig. 8c-d. For CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites, the release of Ag+ was relatively fast at the first 24 h, and then gradually slowed down and reached a stable value, and the cumulative amount of Ag+ released into the solution was 115.45, 117.55, 144.1, and 147.05 μg/L, respectively. The release of Bi3+ was relatively stable, and the cumulative amounts released into the solution reached 35.45, 54.65, 199.1, and 313.05 μg/L, respectively. Therefore, if CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are applied to antimicrobial applications, the toxic damage to organisms may be minimal, and the slow release of Ag+ and Bi3+ from the composites not only kills the bacteria but also makes the antimicrobial materials usable for a long time.

Based on the results and discussion above, a hypothesis regarding the photocatalytic bacteriostasis led by CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite is described schematically in Fig. 9 . As the semiconductor catalyst absorbs light with an energy equal to or greater than the band gap energy, the excitation of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) occurs leaving positive holes in Vb. In the case of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs, upon irradiation of the visible light, electrons in CAU-17/Ag can be excited from VB to CB. Owing to the high specific surface area and rich pore structure of the composite, the photogenerated electrons on the surface of CAU-17/Ag can be easily transferred to CQDs and be stabilized, consequently, the chance for the recombination of electron-hole pair is greatly reduced [82]. Instead, photogenerated electrons and holes may react with oxygen and water in the air to produce large amounts of ROS, •OH, and •O2 −. From the above calculations of CB and VB for Mott-Schottky curves (Figure S5c) and UV–vis DRS (Fig. 7) we know that the CB potentials of CAU-17/Ag and CAU-17/Ag/CQDs are about −0.89 V and −0.68 V, which are more negative than the redox potentials of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 V), [83] suggesting that the photogenerated electrons in the CB of the composites are able to react with O2 thermodynamically reacting to generate •O2 − (2). In addition, the potential for the oxidative reduction of O2 to H2O2 is 0.695 V, [84] and the photogenerated electrons excited into and around the CB can reduce the O2 in solution to H2O2 (3). O2 and H2O2 are formed consecutively by reduction of O2 or by filling two π* orbitals with electrons and then protonating to each orbital, H2O2 dissociates from the OO bond to •OH when it is reduced by further reaction with e- (4). [85] At the same time, the holes left on the VB of CAU-17/Ag/CQDs react with hydroxyl groups to generate •OH (5), which is another way to produce •OH. The final •OH produced reacted with the remaining holes to form 1O2 (6). ESR spectra (Fig. 8) also confirmed the presence of active species •OH and •O2 −. These active substances induced by CAU-17/Ag/CQDs can directly attack bacterial cell membranes, cell envelopes, or intrinsic cell structures to impose a non-specific oxidative stress that potentially leads to cell death. Meanwhile, the penetration of Ag+ into the bacterial cells reinforces the production of free radicals. Under dark conditions, CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composites may establish electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bonds with microbial cell membrane (Gram-negative bacteria), cell wall (Gram-positive bacteria), or protein/DNA that is critical in the intrinsic cell structure, which can cause the cell membrane/wall destruction or malfunctions in cell growth leading to cell lysis [86]. Moreover, Bi3+ and Ag+ released from CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposites can potentially bring cytotoxicity that causes bacterial cell death.
CAU-17/Ag/CQDs + hν → CAU-17/Ag/CQDs(e- + h+)(1）
O2 + e- → •O2 −（2）
•O2 − + H2O → H2O2 + O2 + •OH（3）
H2O2 + e- → •OH + –OH（4）
OH–/H2O + h+ → •OH（5）
•OH + h+ → 1O2（6）

Depicted in Fig. 10 a, CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite exhibits a stronger growth inhibitory against HepG2 cells, than that for non-doping species, CAU-17 (Fig. 10c). The inhibitory rate of HepG2 upon the treatment with CAU-17/Ag/CQDs composite increases with the increase in the composite concentration. As the concentration of the composite reaches 0.8 mg/mL, the inhibitory rate against HepG2 reaches 75 %, which is much larger than that treated with CAU-17 and 5-fluorouracil at the same concentration. The non-linear regression equation fitted (Fig. 10b) resulted in an IC50 value of 0.05 mg/mL for the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposites, and the data showed that the CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposites have the potential to replace 5-fluorouracil as an anti-tumour drug.

In conclusion, we developed a simple method using surfactant PVP as a dispersant to control the dispersion of Ag and CQDs grown on the surface and formed a ternary heterojunction material by solvothermal in-situ growth of Ag and CQDs at the interface of CAU-17, which was doped with Ag and CQDs to give a unique nanosheet-like morphology. photocatalysts indicated a stronger photocatalytic bacterial inhibition activity than CAU-17, wherein, 99.99 % inhibition against S. aureus, E. coli, and MRSA was observed under the visible light irradiation at the concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The improved photocatalytic inhibition activity of the composite was attributed to the unique nanosheet morphology and electronic structure due to the doping of Ag and CQDs, which leads to large interfacial contact area, effective charge transfer, highly efficient electron-hole separation, enhanced light absorption, and extended live promotion of ROS, •OH, and •O2 − production. In addition, the in vitro antitumor tests showed that CAU-17/Ag/CQDs nanocomposite can be an alternative to 5-fluorouracil as an antitumor drug against HepG2 cells. This design strategy will provide new concepts for effluent evolution, photodynamic therapy, and oncology treatment, and the product can be applied to cancer therapy, drug delivery, and microbial disinfection of different taxonomic groups.
